subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: All five sections are organised around producing deductive arguments: section 6.2 gives visual proofs that sums of odd/even numbers must have specific parity; section 6.3 derives that the magic sum must be 15 and the centre must be 5 by necessity; section 6.4 uses a recursive argument to establish the Virahāṅka sequence; section 6.5 eliminates digit assignments through logical deduction in cryptarithms. Producing convincing arguments is the chapter's unifying purpose.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Section 6.2 derives algebraic rules for the nth even number (2n) and nth odd number (2n-1) and explores parity patterns in expressions; section 6.4 identifies the repeating O-O-E parity pattern in the Virahāṅka sequence — substantively engaging the pattern-discovery and rule-description strand.

Section 6.3 constructs a fully generalised 3×3 magic square using algebraic expressions (m, m+1, m-3, m+4, etc.) to prove that any entry can be expressed in terms of the centre m, and uses this form to build magic squares for arbitrary centre values and magic sums.

Section 6.3 explicitly presents Indian mathematical history through the Chautīsā Yantra (Khajuraho, 10th century CE), Lo Shu, Navagraha and Kubera Yantras; section 6.4 establishes Virahāṅka (c. 700 CE) as the originator of the Fibonacci-type sequence, predating Leonardo of Pisa by five centuries.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to reason deductively about number properties by applying logical argumentation — parity proofs, algebraic magic-square derivations, and cryptarithm elimination — to produce convincing mathematical arguments.
